title: Dosa

overview.description: Thin, crisp South Indian fermented rice and lentil pancakes with a delicate tang. Traditionally served with sambar and coconut chutney.

ingredients: Vegetable oil or ghee, for cooking
method: Wash the rice until the water runs clear.
Soak the rice and poha together for 6–8 hours.
Soak the urad dal and fenugreek seeds separately for 6–8 hours.
Drain both mixtures, reserving some soaking water.
Blend the urad dal until very smooth, adding just enough water to keep it moving.
Blend the rice until smooth with a slight grainy texture.
Combine both batters in a large bowl.
Mix thoroughly using your hand or a spoon.
Cover and leave to ferment for 8–12 hours until risen and bubbly.
Heat a cast-iron or non-stick pan over a medium-high heat.
Lightly wipe with a little oil.
Pour a ladle of batter into the centre.
Spread quickly in a spiral from the centre outwards using the base of the ladle.
Drizzle a little oil or ghee around the edge.
Cook until the underside is crisp and golden.
Fold or roll and serve immediately.

learning.did-you-know: The fermentation process gives dosa its distinctive flavour while also making the nutrients easier to digest.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to ferment dosa batter, achieve the correct consistency and spread thin, crisp dosas.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: An authentic South Indian staple that makes excellent use of pantry ingredients already stocked in the Chapman Kitchen.
chapman-tips: A slightly grainy rice batter produces a crispier dosa.
Fermentation time depends on room temperature—look for a bubbly, airy batter rather than watching the clock.
Your food processor or blender works very well for home dosa batter.
If the batter becomes too thick after fermentation, loosen it with a little water before cooking.
notes.batch-cooking: Freeze fermented batter in meal-sized portions.
notes.leftovers: The batter keeps in the fridge for 3–4 days and can also be used for uttapam.
